claim: Per-section kg/lbs tabs will feel natural to Jake and he will not ask to revert to a single global toggle
basis: He liked the idea when proposed; PT workflow genuinely benefits from independent section units

claim: MFP CSV import will be the feature that makes the macros panel actually useful day-to-day for Jake
basis: Macros panel currently stores targets only; MFP is the most practical source of actual intake data without a backend

claim: Session logging will be the next major feature request after MFP import
basis: Identified as biggest missing PT workflow feature in mid-session review; Jake agreed persistence was priority first

claim: The per-client calendar approach will hold long-term â€” no pressure to move schedule back to the dashboard
basis: Jake himself raised the information-overload concern and approved moving the grid to profile-only; this matches how PTs actually work (open client, review session plan)

claim: Workout Builder will be the highest-effort single feature in Tier 2 and will take more than one session to complete cleanly
basis: TrainHeroic-style template system requires template CRUD, exercise library, set/rep/rest schema, and a separate log-session flow â€” that is at least three distinct UI surfaces

claim: Program Templates will require a dedicated design session before build â€” the data model (global programs vs per-client assignments, week/day slot structure) has enough surface area that building without a spec will cause rework
basis: Spec filed this session; multiple dependency touch-points: calendar events, My Progress filter, template reuse â€” rushing build risks a schema that doesn't compose
outcome: correct â€” design walk-through session held before build; three key decisions surfaced (global library, day slots, auto-events) that would have required rework if assumed
score_note: prediction verified same session; the walk-through directly shaped the data model

claim: The global program library approach will hold â€” PTs won't need per-client program variants often enough to justify a fork feature in v1
basis: Jake approved Option A without hesitation; typical PT workflow runs the same program (PPL, 5/3/1) across multiple clients with minor load adjustments, not structural differences

claim: The sort+filter bar (Name/Weight Change/Last Session + Losing/Gaining/No Goal/Today pills) will satisfy Jake and not be trimmed
basis: Option 3 was presented as a clean UX improvement and Jake selected it
outcome: incorrect ï¿½ Jake trimmed to name-only sort immediately after seeing it rendered
score_note: presented too many controls; Jake wanted the minimum viable sort, not a full filter system

claim: GitHub Pages will hold long-term as the hosting solution â€” Jake will not request a backend or dynamic hosting within the next 3 months
basis: Jake explicitly asked for permanent hosting to avoid regenerating from Claude; static hosting fits the single-file architecture; no feature on the roadmap requires a server

claim: The post-migration stale-reference pattern will recur when Program Builder migrates to the new phases model â€” a grep for old references before declaring migration done will prevent at least one session of bug-fixing
basis: Three separate bugs this session all traced to the global sessions migration leaving stale references in functions not updated at migration time

claim: The Log Session modal will require at least one more session of refinement (exercise add, form validation, save feedback) before Jake considers it complete
basis: The modal was only rendered with injected demo data in the preview; save round-trip unverified; exercise add-from-search not yet built; Jake's pattern is to see it working then immediately request the next gap

claim: Jake's in-Claude-Code preview workflow will become the primary iteration loop for PTHub UI work â€” he won't revert to browser-based review
basis: He explicitly set this up: 'so I can make suggestions and changes to you without going to my browser' â€” deliberate workflow design, not a one-off. Aligns with his ship-small instinct: see it, change it, see it again.

claim: The CoachApp set form rebuild will be confirmed working on next session start â€” the code is correct but Jake couldn't verify due to browser cache
basis: All code changes are in the file; the only failure was browser cache. Hard refresh should resolve it.
outcome: correct — set form confirmed working; listed in STATUS verified features since June

claim: Jake will not return to building in PTHub â€” the decision to freeze it and focus on CoachApp will hold
basis: Jake stated it clearly and unprompted: 'i want coachapp to be the new project.' No ambiguity. PTHub was causing him frustration by splitting his attention.

claim: Weight tracking will be the first CoachApp feature built next session â€” the table exists and it was agreed as priority 1
basis: Supabase weight_logs table already exists. Jake approved the migration order. No blockers.
outcome: correct — weight tracking built and in STATUS verified working list (log, chart, stats row)

claim: The three-account-type model (coach/client/solo, coach_id nullable) will hold without structural changes through the weight tracking and invite system builds
basis: The model is internally consistent: solo = client with null coach_id. No data model conflicts anticipated for the next two features.

claim: Jake will want to build the client-facing experience sooner than the migration order implies â€” once he sees the invite system working, he'll want to see what the client sees
basis: He stated the client dashboard requirements with as much detail as the PT dashboard. His instinct is to see the full picture. The invite system will trigger the question 'what does the client see when they log in?'

claim: The Supabase RLS policies will need to be revisited when client accounts go live â€” current policies assume coach-only auth
basis: All existing RLS policies are written around coach auth.uid(). Client login will fail or see wrong data unless each table's policy is extended to include client access. This is a known gap, not a surprise.
outcome: correct â€” 11 client SELECT policies added this session; also discovered and fixed broken policy that was querying auth.users directly

claim: The user_id stamping bug is caused by `db.auth.updateUser()` returning a null or incomplete user object in the invite flow context â€” the fix will be to call `db.auth.getUser()` separately after updateUser rather than relying on the updateUser return value
basis: Three stamping approaches all failed despite correct SQL. The common factor is the frontend passing the user object. If user is null, the RPC gets null params and silently does nothing.
score_note: Root cause was different: Edge Function discarded inviteUserByEmail return value (user.id) and never stamped user_id at send time. Triggers failed silently due to FK ordering and row_security issues. Fix: Edge Function stamps using returned user.id with service role. Frontend updateUser call was fine throughout.

claim: The invite system will now work end-to-end without further fixes â€” user_id is stamped at invite-send time via Edge Function, client dashboard loads on acceptance
basis: Confirmed in testing: user_id populated before client clicked link; client landed on dashboard after accepting invite

claim: Jake will want client-facing dashboard sooner rather than later now that the invite system is confirmed working â€” seeing clients able to log in will trigger 'what do they actually see?'
basis: Invite now works end-to-end. Jake's pattern: once infrastructure works he immediately thinks about the user experience on the other side. He mentioned client dashboard in the blueprint with specific detail.

claim: The 'going in circles' protocol (web search audit before more code) will prevent at least one wasted debug session in the next 5 sessions
basis: This session lost significant time because we kept trying frontend fixes before reading the Supabase docs on inviteUserByEmail return value. The answer was in the docs.

claim: The hello-claude skill will auto-load correctly from C:\Users\jaken\.claude\skills\hello-claude\SKILL.md at next session start
basis: Claude Code discovers skills from nested .claude/skills/ directories â€” but global user-level .claude skills discovery is less documented. Created the file; auto-load unverified.
outcome: file confirmed present with correct frontmatter; auto-load behaviour to be confirmed at next session start

claim: Having git set up will mean Jake runs /code-review before at least half of Netlify deploys going forward
basis: Jake responded positively to the suggestion and immediately agreed to git init. Whether the habit sticks depends on session momentum â€” easy to skip when excited to deploy.

claim: The three client header buttons (Update email, Update details, Resend invite) will work correctly when tested in browser â€” no logic bugs in the code
basis: Code is straightforward: showUpdateEmailModal is a new function with one field, sendClientInvite is unchanged, showEditClientModal is unchanged. invited_at drives the Send vs Resend state.
outcome: correct â€” verified in preview: all three buttons present, Update email modal opens with correct pre-filled value

claim: Jake will want the PT dashboard built before programs â€” it's the feature that makes the product feel real as a daily tool
basis: He asked 'what do you think is next' after performance â€” deferred to my recommendation. Dashboard is the Monday morning view he described in detail early in the session. Programs are structural, dashboard is visceral.

claim: The Netlify free tier will be sufficient for v1 â€” static files only, no serverless functions needed on Netlify side (Edge Functions are on Supabase)
basis: CoachApp is a pure static site. All backend logic is in Supabase. Netlify just serves HTML/CSS/JS.

claim: Jake will want to work on the client-facing dashboard next session â€” he explicitly identified it as next priority and the RLS foundations are now in place
basis: He said 'I would like to spend time building solid foundations before adding new features' â€” once foundations are done, client dashboard is the named next step in the roadmap

claim: The sql-safety skill will be invoked before at least 2 of the next 5 SQL operations â€” reducing failed query iterations from average 3 to average 1
basis: Jake explicitly asked for the skill to be created after repeated SQL failures. He's motivated to use it. The skill auto-loads on SQL context. Previous failure pattern was entirely caused by not checking schema/constraints first.

claim: The client dashboard will require RLS write policies for at least weight_logs and workout_logs before it's fully functional â€” clients need to log their own data
basis: Current client policies are SELECT-only. The client dashboard roadmap includes 'client logs own weight' and 'client logs own workouts'. Without INSERT policies these features cannot function.
outcome: correct â€” write policies added this session; client self-logging confirmed working

claim: Jake will test the workout runner at the gym tomorrow and return with specific UX feedback â€” particularly around the flow between exercises and the rest timer
basis: He explicitly said he wants to test on his phone in the gym tomorrow. First real-world test of the runner will surface gaps that preview testing doesn't catch.
outcome: correct — 2026-07-02 gym test produced a specific bug report with screenshots (rest-timer double-display, phantom set), later than the predicted date but exactly the flow/rest-timer areas called

claim: The mobile-check habit will prevent the next sidebar-invisible bug â€” the skill + memory combination means the dual-surface rule will be checked before reporting any UI change done
basis: The rule is now in persistent memory AND as a skill file. But habits take repetition to stick â€” one session is not enough to confirm.

claim: Jake will want a rest timer in the workout runner within the next 2 sessions â€” it's the most obvious missing feature once he uses it in a real gym session
basis: The reference app he showed (Hevy) has a prominent rest timer. He'll notice its absence when he finishes a set and has nothing tracking his rest interval.

claim: Push A and pull a templates will remain empty until Jake explicitly adds exercises via the workout builder â€” the runner will show a blank exercise name for those templates
basis: Both templates have 0 exercises in DB. Runner loads exercises from template. Blank name is the current behaviour. Jake hasn't been told to add them yet.
outcome: correct — confirmed 2026-07-01 live test: no standalone template on the E2E coach account has any exercises attached

claim: The pre-push hook will catch at least one real bug before it reaches GitHub Pages in the next 10 pushes
basis: The hook already surfaced a duplicate function false-positive on first run, proving it executes. The patterns it checks (wrong columns, unscoped queries) have each appeared at least once in real code this project.

claim: The hello-claude code review step (Step 4) will surface at least one issue per session for the next 3 sessions before the codebase stabilises
basis: Bug count has been ~7-10 per audit. The hook + session review double-layer is new â€” some issues will have been missed by the hook and caught at session start instead.

claim: Jake will ask to automate more of the review/quality process â€” the pre-push hook will feel like the right direction and he'll want it extended or applied elsewhere
basis: His response to 'is there a way for you to run these checks without me prompting?' was immediately positive and led to implementing both layers. Pattern: once he sees a system working, he extends it.

claim: openWorkoutLog two-query fix will work correctly in production â€” session history views will load for real users
basis: Logic is sound: two separate queries avoid the PGRST200, manual merge is straightforward. The only failure path is if workout_log_exercises returns null for a valid logId, which would be an RLS issue not a query issue.

claim: The auth re-render guard fix will prevent the mid-session dashboard snap for real users â€” token refreshes will no longer call showApp()
basis: The fix is a one-line condition change from '!_appLoaded || SIGNED_IN' to '!_appLoaded'. Token refreshes fire SIGNED_IN with _appLoaded=true, so showApp() is skipped. Genuine sign-outs set _appLoaded=false, so subsequent SIGNED_IN correctly re-bootstraps.

claim: Jake will ask for a fuller dbq() sweep across all ~15 remaining unguarded queries within the next 2 sessions â€” once he understands the value of the wrapper, he'll want it everywhere
basis: His pattern: once a system improvement lands and he sees it working, he asks to extend it everywhere ('do both', 'do it all'). He already asked about more error tracking this session.

claim: The preview browser stale state issue is isolated to this session â€” next session start with a fresh browser context will load cleanly
basis: The repeated SIGNED_IN loop was caused by eval calls triggering Supabase token events in a polluted browser session. Fresh server start next session will not carry that state.
outcome: correct — the SIGNED_IN loop never recurred; later preview issues were a different class (stale launch.json path)

claim: The GitHub Actions CI will go live within the next session â€” Jake will add workflow scope to his PAT and push, and the Actions tab will show a passing run
basis: Jake had the PAT page open and understood exactly what to do; single checkbox to tick

claim: The missing client INSERT policy on workout_log_sets will turn out to have been silently blocking client self-logging â€” once Jake tests client self-log post-RLS-migration, he'll confirm sets were previously not saving
basis: The policy gap was structural â€” no row in workout_log_sets could be inserted by a client JWT. However it's possible client self-logging hasn't been tested end-to-end yet so the bug may not have surfaced visibly.

claim: Jake will ask to implement Realtime client notifications (coach assigns program â†’ client sees it without refresh) within the next 2 sessions
basis: It appeared on the STATUS priority list as item 4; Jake approved researching Realtime this session; the pattern shows he moves to build as soon as foundations are solid

claim: The (SELECT auth.uid()) RLS optimisation will produce a measurable improvement in page load time on the client dashboard and workout log views â€” these pages do the most policy-evaluated queries
basis: Supabase docs cite 94.97% improvement per-policy evaluation; CoachApp's deepest queries hit workout_log_sets through 3 joined policies. The compounding effect should be visible on slow connections.

claim: Jake will continue actively auditing and expanding the memory system â€” he will suggest additional memory entries or categories within the next 3 sessions
basis: He proactively suggested adding user profile, dual explanations, review, verify, skill creator, and code review to memory unprompted. This is not a one-off; it matches his pattern of building permanent systems to eliminate recurring friction.

claim: The post-save navigation fix (v80) will be confirmed correct â€” client saves session and lands on Workouts page, not PT profile
basis: The fix is a one-line role check; currentView is set correctly by switchView(); navigate(workouts) is a standard call used throughout
outcome: correct — role-aware post-save navigation verified; in STATUS working list

claim: Once client session history is tappable, Jake will identify the session log view as needing a redesign â€” no summary stats, no comparison to previous, RPE column empty
basis: We already identified these gaps in the live test; Jake explicitly wanted to see the logging UI in real life and the view is visually sparse

claim: The two-query workaround in openWorkoutLog can be safely replaced with a nested join now that the FK constraint is confirmed present â€” no rework risk
basis: FK constraint existence confirmed by SQL; PostgREST nested join requires FK; the constraint is the only blocker that required the workaround

claim: Jake will ask for a session summary / stats header at the top of the workout log view â€” total volume, sets, duration â€” within the next 1-2 sessions
basis: He ran the E2E test specifically to see the logging UI; the current view has no headline stats; the runner finish screen already has this pattern so he knows it is possible

claim: The daily question cron will need to be recreated at most once per week â€” Jake starts Claude Code most working days and the 7-day auto-expiry aligns with that cadence
basis: Jake's pattern is frequent short sessions; cron is session-only so it lives as long as Code stays open or is restarted within a session day

claim: The Playwright suite will catch at least one real regression before the July beta â€” the runner and client session flow are the most likely areas
basis: Both areas had bugs found only via live E2E testing (post-save navigation, tappable rows); those paths are now covered by automated tests; future changes to the runner or client workouts page will hit the suite first

claim: Jake will ask to expand the daily question list or adjust the questions after seeing the first few â€” the current list is calibrated for week 3 but he'll have opinions on tone or topic balance
basis: He's hands-on about how the collaboration works; asked specifically about personality vs work balance; likely to redirect if the questions don't feel right

claim: The hello-claude to-do verification step will prevent stale items from appearing at the next session start â€” Step 7 will find nothing to carry forward
basis: Both known stale items (PAT scope, verify v80) have been removed. The verification logic now runs gh run list and checks Playwright coverage before surfacing any item. If STATUS.md is clean and CI is passing, the step will return an empty list.
outcome: correct — no stale item has reappeared at a session start since; carried items were all genuinely open

claim: The Programs feature will surface at least 2 UX issues during next-session E2E testing that were not visible in the preview â€” likely around phase-week calculation, day-of-week edge cases, or the assign-to-client flow
basis: The program card in client dashboard uses relative date math (weeks since start date) and day-of-week mapping; these edge cases only surface with real data and real dates. Preview was only tested at one point in time.

claim: The modal rendering rule (dynamic creation at body level, class=modal not modal-box) will be violated at least once more in the next 5 builds if not enforced by the pre-push hook
basis: The same mistake (static modal in el.innerHTML) was made twice this session â€” once intentionally designed that way, once caught only by Jake reporting a visible bug. Without a hook that scans for modal-box class or static overlay HTML inside page renders, the pattern will recur.

claim: Jake will want the Programs client view to show the current week plan (which workout is today, what is coming up this week) rather than just a list of templates
basis: He built a full Hyrox Week 1 and the client card expand is now useful — but there is no view that says today is Monday, your workout is Row Threshold. The Programs to calendar integration is on the roadmap and Jake moves fast once one piece is working.

claim: The expandable workout card pattern will become the default for other client-facing list views — e.g. session history will get a similar expand for quick stats without navigating away
basis: Jake immediately liked the expand when he saw it. His pattern: once a UX pattern lands well he extends it to equivalent surfaces. Session history cards currently navigate to a separate view — expand-to-preview is a natural complement.

claim: pth-033 was correct — rest timer was built within 2 sessions of the gym test
outcome: correct — rest timer built in v77-v78, same session window
score_note: confirmed correct; built as part of cardio runner interval timer

claim: My Progress Strength tab will hit a PostgREST filter syntax issue on live — the workout_logs!inner join with .eq filter may not filter by client_id correctly and will return empty or over-scoped data
basis: PostgREST embedded filter syntax on !inner joins can behave differently from preview testing. If the Strength tab returns zero rows on live, this is the first place to look.

claim: Personal Bests tab in My Progress will fail for client users due to missing client-scoped RLS policy on performance_logs
basis: performance_logs has coach-scoped RLS only. A client SELECT policy was never explicitly added. Client login will get a permission error or zero rows on the PBs tab.

claim: Jake will confirm the last session strip above the stats bar as the right UX placement after testing live — persistent reference during a set is genuinely useful mid-workout
basis: Jake explicitly chose persistent placement (said 1. persistent) over in-empty-state. Compact horizontal strip is the agreed final form.

claim: Client calendar tab and day detail modal will work correctly on live site without further fixes
basis: Code is sound â€” programWorkoutsByDate mapping was already working for the calendar grid; day detail modal is simple DOM injection reading window._calProgramWorkouts. Main risk is if clientId fetch fails for an edge case.
score_note: Smoke tested in preview v143 — all checks PASS. Not confirmed on live site. Extended to pth-062.

claim: The discuss-then-approve working agreement will hold for the next 3+ sessions â€” Jake values it enough to maintain the discipline himself
basis: Jake explicitly asked for it, explained why (partial info causes rework), and framed it as a rule not a suggestion. He enforces his own process preferences consistently once stated.

claim: The 12-week program will surface UX gaps in the program/phase view (e.g. browsing week templates by phase rather than flat list) within 2 sessions of Jake using it as a client
basis: 44 week templates are now hidden from the flat list â€” which is correct â€” but the phase view in Programs has not been updated to surface them well. Jake will want to see Week 7 at a glance.

claim: The Playwright accordion tests will be effectively no-ops until the test client account is assigned a program — they pass by skipping, not by asserting
basis: Tests check for a button containing /session/ to determine if accordion is present. The Playwright CLIENT_EMAIL account has no program assigned, so the tests always skip. They provide false coverage confidence.

claim: Client notes will fail silently on first use after push — the SQL migration must be run before the feature works, and Jake has not yet run it
basis: saveRunnerSession inserts client_notes into workout_log_exercises. The column does not exist in production yet. Supabase will return a 42703 error. The session save itself will succeed (partial data only).

claim: Client calendar and day detail modal will be confirmed working on live site without further fixes â€” consistent with preview smoke test
basis: Smoke test in preview v143 passed all checks; code is sound; only risk is if clientId fetch fails for an edge case on live Supabase

claim: Edit start date will be the first feature a real PT requests be changed â€” the lack of a confirmation step risks accidental date shifts that confuse clients mid-program
basis: Shifting a start date silently moves all scheduled workouts. If a client has already logged sessions, the calendar shift could confuse them. No confirmation step currently exists.

claim: The UI consistency pass (SESSION N/M labels + exercise list on all three surfaces) will not require a revisit
basis: Jake explicitly said he preferred the PT UI and approved the consistency pass; pattern applied identically across PT Programs, client calendar modal, and client Workouts page

claim: Jake will confirm the Test Client invite from the live site rather than revisiting it from localhost
basis: Response to localhost timeout was pragmatic deferral not escalation; he accepted the master-account workaround for client-side E2E without complaint

claim: The stale-path preview bug will not recur - worktrees created after 2026-07-02 will serve the correct OneDrive/coachapp path, because the parent seed template (Claude/.claude/launch.json) was fixed, not just the active worktree
basis: Root cause was the parent template that seeds every new worktree; it and all 18 existing copies were corrected and verified. Prior 3 occurrences only ever patched the per-worktree copy.

claim: The next LLM wiki ingest will consult wiki/sources.md and skip already-processed sources instead of re-reading them, cutting read volume versus this session
basis: Manifest created and wired as read-first/update-last into the wiki CLAUDE.md workflow and the wiki-ingest skill; but it only pays off if the skill is actually used and the manifest kept complete

claim: Bare /save keeps dispatching to the CoachApp skill across future sessions with no regression, and hello-claude Step 9 surfaces any golden-path drift automatically
basis: Framework command renamed to /vault-save and committed (f6f3c48) so worktree checkouts inherit it; self-verified once this session (this very /save dispatched correctly); daily find-sweep added to hello-claude Step 9
